CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early and carried through the method and experiments, making the narrative easy to follow. The writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is adequate, but the summary lacks mention of clearly defined notation and explicit reporting of hyperparameters, and it is unclear whether figures/tables fully illuminate the novel cognitive features. These minor presentation omissions prevent a top score but do not impede understanding of the core ideas. | B(4): Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (augmenting textual features with eye-tracking based cognitive features and empirical F-score gains) are stated early and carried through method and experiments involving SVM/NB/RB and multiple datasets. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, so the exposition is generally clear for an applied NLP audience interested in cognitive signals. Minor presentation gaps remain: the manuscript does not report hyperparameters or code availability and gives limited detail on figures/tables and notation for the novel saliency-graph features, which could hinder reproducibility and make some methodological descriptions harder to follow.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early and carried through the method and experiments, making the narrative easy to follow. The writing quality is described as good and related work coverage is adequate, but the summary lacks mention of clearly defined notation and explicit reporting of hyperparameters, and it is unclear whether figures/tables fully illuminate the novel cognitive features. These minor presentation omissions prevent a top score but do not impede understanding of the core ideas.

reviewer_b.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and the main contributions (augmenting textual features with eye-tracking based cognitive features and empirical F-score gains) are stated early and carried through method and experiments involving SVM/NB/RB and multiple datasets. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate, so the exposition is generally clear for an applied NLP audience interested in cognitive signals. Minor presentation gaps remain: the manuscript does not report hyperparameters or code availability and gives limited detail on figures/tables and notation for the novel saliency-graph features, which could hinder reproducibility and make some methodological descriptions harder to follow.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper demonstrates consistent empirical gains from adding eye-tracking cognitive features but falls short on methodological depth and reproducibility: missing ablation/sensitivity analyses, statistical testing, detailed hyperparameters, and released code/data. Given the solid but limited empirical evidence and the significant gaps that prevent attributing gains to specific cognitive features or fully reproducing results, a middle score reflects promise but incomplete substantiation.

reviewer_a.justification: The evaluation shows empirical gains on two datasets, but key methodological analyses are missing: there are no reported ablation studies isolating individual cognitive feature types nor sensitivity analyses or statistical tests to support claimed improvements. Reproducibility is weak—no code, raw eye-tracking data, hyperparameters, or detailed collection protocol are provided—and aggregation of gaze signals masks subject-level variability that the authors themselves note. Related work is adequately covered and some limitations are discussed, but the experimental depth required to convincingly attribute gains to the proposed cognitive features is lacking.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper presents consistent empirical gains from adding eye-tracking cognitive features across multiple classifiers and a held-out dataset, which supports the central claim that behavioral signals help sentiment analysis. However, the summary does not mention ablation or sensitivity analyses (e.g., which cognitive feature groups drive gains), hyperparameter reporting, or released code, limiting reproducibility and deeper understanding of what components are essential. Related work coverage is reported as adequate and the authors discuss practical limitations (need for eye-tracking data, hardware/tool errors), which is good. Overall the experiments are adequate to demonstrate promise but miss important analyses and reproduction details that would make the evidence comprehensive.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes a novel and meaningful contribution by integrating gaze-derived cognitive features into sentiment and sarcasm detection and reports consistent empirical gains across classifiers and held-out data. Practical limitations—dependence on eye-tracking data, scalability, and lack of clear release/replication materials—temper immediate broad adoption, but the demonstrated improvements and the new research direction justify a strong impact score of 4.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a meaningful problem—improving sentiment analysis by incorporating cognitive (eye-tracking) features—and reports nontrivial gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3% F-score) and better handling of complex phenomena like sarcasm. However, practical impact is constrained because the approach depends on eye-tracking data availability, and the summary does not indicate release of code, raw gaze data, or detailed protocols needed for adoption and replication. It opens useful new directions linking psycholinguistics and NLP, but benefits appear moderate and contingent on resolving reproducibility and data-collection scalability.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper tackles an important challenge—improving sentiment and sarcasm detection—by integrating cognitive (eye-movement) features and demonstrates consistent F-score gains (up to 3.7% and 9.3%) across multiple classifiers and a held-out dataset. As the first work to incorporate gaze-derived features for sentiment, it meaningfully bridges cognitive science and NLP and opens clear new research directions (multimodal behavioral signals, targeted handling of irony/thwarting). Practical limitations (need for eye-tracking data, aggregation issues) temper immediate broad adoption, but the demonstrated empirical gains make this a strong-impact contribution likely to influence future work.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper is squarely within the venue’s NLP scope and addresses sentiment analysis with a novel multimodal (cognitive/eye-tracking) contribution and standard evaluations. I rate it a 4 because while the work is relevant and likely of strong interest to the community, practical and reproducibility concerns (specialized gaze data, missing code/hyperparameter/ablation details) limit its broader impact and prevent a top score without addressing those issues.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic (augmenting sentiment analysis with cognitive/eye-tracking features) lies squarely within NLP and could interest the venue’s audience, and the problem formulation and standard evaluation metrics are appropriate. However, the approach depends on specialized gaze data and the paper does not mention released code or hyperparameters; together with the reported aggregation of participant signals and lack of clear ablation/reproducibility details, this limits the practical impact and broader applicability that top-tier venues expect. Given these scope and reproducibility concerns, the paper is borderline appropriate rather than a clear fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within the scope of top NLP venues: it targets sentiment analysis while introducing cognitive (eye-tracking) features, a clear multimodal contribution that bridges cognitive science and NLP. The problem formulation and evaluation (F-score improvements on two datasets, held-out tests, multiple classifiers) meet community standards, and the demonstrated gains on hard phenomena (irony, sarcasm, thwarting) make it highly relevant to researchers interested in novel modality integration. Practicality limitations (need for gaze data) are acknowledged but do not undermine the paper’s relevance or audience fit for a top conference.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper introduces a meaningful and underexplored integration of eye-tracking–derived cognitive features into sentiment analysis, yielding consistent empirical gains and offering a novel modality coupling. However, they also agree the contribution is primarily feature engineering plugged into standard classifiers rather than a new algorithmic or theoretical framework, so the novelty is substantial but not transformative—hence a score reflecting notable (but incremental) originality.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper advances a noteworthy perspective by incorporating eye-tracking derived cognitive features into sentiment analysis—an idea the authors claim is novel and, based on the summary, appears to be the first application of gaze features to this task. However, the contribution is primarily feature engineering plugged into off-the-shelf classifiers (SVM, NB, rule-based) without a new algorithmic or theoretical framework, so the technical novelty is limited. The work differentiates itself from prior text-only systems and shows empirical gains on two datasets, but as an NLP researcher I would view this as a moderate, incremental novelty rather than a significant methodological advance.

reviewer_b.justification: From my perspective as an applied cognitive NLP researcher, integrating eye-movement–derived cognitive features (fixations, saccades, regressions, saliency-graph metrics) into sentiment classifiers represents a notable and underexplored modality coupling that is plausibly the first of its kind for sentiment analysis as the authors claim. The work clearly differentiates itself from prior text-only systems (evaluated on Mishra et al. 2016 and Joshi et al. 2014, with consistent F-score gains across SVM/NB/RB and held-out tests), so it offers a meaningful new perspective. That said, the technical contribution is primarily feature-augmentation rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic framework, so the novelty is significant but not radical. Overall this is a solid, notable novelty in modality integration rather than a paradigm shift.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the experiments show empirical improvements from adding eye-tracking features across multiple classifiers and a held-out dataset, supporting the core claim. However, missing analyses (no ablations isolating features), lack of statistical-significance testing, absent hyperparameter/code release, and concerns about aggregated gaze signals and hardware errors weaken reproducibility and full technical validation. Given solid but incompletely validated evidence, a middle score of 3 reflects plausible results that require further methodological rigor to be fully sound.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper reports empirical improvements from adding eye-tracking–derived cognitive features and evaluates across multiple classifiers and two datasets, so the main experimental claim is provisionally supported. However, the summary indicates no reported ablation studies isolating individual cognitive features, no statistical-significance testing or hyperparameter details, and code/data release is not mentioned, undermining reproducibility. The authors themselves note aggregation of participant signals (potentially losing informative individual-level patterns) and hardware/tool errors, which are not fully controlled for. Given these methodological gaps and missing analyses, the technical claims are plausible but not yet convincingly or fully validated.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and presents experimental evidence of F-score improvements (up to 3.7% and 9.3%) across multiple classifiers and a held-out dataset, which supports the core technical claim that eye-tracking features can improve sentiment classification. However, some methodological details are missing (no hyperparameter reporting, code availability, or explicit mention of statistical significance testing) and the acknowledged aggregation of gaze data raises questions about loss of individual-level signal. Overall the experiments and baselines are reasonable and results largely justify the conclusions, but minor reporting gaps prevent a flawless technical assessment.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper evaluates against sensible traditional baselines on established datasets and reports standard metrics, but both note important omissions: lack of contemporary neural/state-of-the-art baselines, limited ablation to isolate cognitive features, and missing hyperparameter/reproducibility details. Because the comparisons are reasonable but incomplete and limit claims about real gains versus modern methods, a middling score of 3 reflects adequate but insufficiently comprehensive comparison.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against sensible traditional baselines (textual-feature SVM/NB and an in-house rule-based system) on the same datasets (Mishra et al., 2016; Joshi et al., 2014) using standard metrics, which is appropriate but limited. From my perspective, important modern baselines (e.g., recent neural/representation learning approaches for sentiment/sarcasm) are not mentioned, hyperparameters and reproducibility details are missing, and it’s unclear whether comparisons control for data augmentation from external corpora. The authors do report when prior methods perform comparably and discuss related work, but the analysis of differences lacks depth and ablation rigor required to isolate the cognitive features’ contribution.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares its cognitive-augmented system against reasonable traditional baselines (textual feature systems using SVM, Naive Bayes and a rule-based classifier) on established datasets (Mishra et al., Joshi et al.) and reports the same metrics (F-score, accuracy), including held-out tests. It candidly notes limitations and discusses prior work adequately, but it does not appear to include more recent or strong neural/state-of-the-art sentiment baselines (e.g., modern deep models) or report hyperparameter/code details, so comparisons are good but omit some important contemporary baselines.

REPLICABILITY.justification: Both reviewers agree that public text datasets and standard evaluation metrics are provided but key implementation details (hyperparameters, model configurations, and released code) and the precise eye‑tracking preprocessing/aggregation pipeline are not specified. These omissions prevent straightforward, exact replication, but because the base datasets and high‑level procedures are available, reproduction is possible with substantive effort and assumptions. Therefore the work is judged partially reproducible.

reviewer_a.justification: From the summary key experimental details are missing: hyperparameters and model configurations are not reported and code is not mentioned, hampering exact reimplementation. While source text datasets (Mishra et al., Joshi et al., Pang & Lee, Sentiment140) are identifiable, the availability and preprocessing of the eye‑tracking (gaze) data and exact aggregation procedures are not specified, and raw eye‑tracking recordings or collection protocol are not stated as released. Evaluation metrics are standard (F‑score, accuracy) but that alone does not rescue reproducibility given the missing training/configuration and data-release details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper lists datasets (Mishra et al. 2016, Joshi et al. 2014, Pang & Lee 2004, Sentiment140) and evaluation metrics (F-score, accuracy, Fleiss kappa), but crucial implementation details are missing: hyperparameters and model configuration choices are not reported and code is not provided. The eye-tracking preprocessing/aggregation is discussed at a high level (e.g., averaging graph features) but the exact pipeline and feature extraction parameters are not specified, making faithful reimplementation difficult. Given public datasets and clear evaluation procedures, reproduction is feasible with effort but important details are absent, so I judge it partially reproducible.

main_claims: Augmenting traditional textual features with cognitive features derived from readers' eye-movement patterns improves sentiment polarity detection.
Using the enhanced feature set yields up to 3.7% and 9.3% F-score improvements on two different datasets compared to systems using only traditional features.
Cognitive (eye-tracking) features help handle complex constructs such as irony, sarcasm and thwarting, with significant improvement across classifiers and on a held-out dataset.
This approach is the first of its kind (to the authors' knowledge) to incorporate eye-movement derived cognitive features into sentiment analysis.
_summary.methodology: Augment traditional sentiment and sarcasm-related textual features with cognitive features extracted from eye-tracking data (fixations, saccades, regressions and saliency-graph based features), then perform statistical classification using classifiers such as SVM, Naive Bayes and an in-house rule-based system to predict binary sentiment polarity.
datasets: Additional training snippets collected from Pang and Lee (2004) and Sentiment140 (10662 snippets) for training some systems
limitations: Requires availability of eye-tracking (gaze) data from human readers, raising practicability concerns.
Errors arise from eyetracker hardware limitations and from linguistic tool/resource errors.
Aggregation of eye-tracking parameters (e.g., averaging graph features across participants) may cause loss of informative individual-level patterns.

reviewer_a.name: Skeptical Theorist
reviewer_a.background: Senior NLP researcher with a decade of experience in computational semantics and model interpretability at a top CS department; multiple ACL/EMNLP program committee memberships.
traits: Penalises papers that lack a clear theoretical or algorithmic contribution beyond feature engineering (will reduce novelty score if no new model or theory is proposed)
Requires thorough ablation studies for each new feature type (will deduct points if cognitive features are not individually ablated and statistically tested)
Demands reproducibility: deducts if code, raw eye-tracking data or detailed collection protocol are not publicly released

Penalises small or non-representative datasets and insufficient analysis of inter-subject variability (will lower significance/generalizability score if sample sizes or subject-level analyses are missing)
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on whether the paper advances core methodology or theory and whether empirical claims are convincingly isolated and reproducible; will be strict about missing ablations, theory, and dataset rigor.

reviewer_b.name: Cognitive NLP Enthusiast
reviewer_b.background: Applied NLP researcher with strong experience in multimodal and cognitive signal integration (eye-tracking, EEG) for language understanding; has published applied systems at ACL/EMNLP showing gains from behavioral data.
traits: Rewards novelty in incorporating behavioral/cognitive modalities (will increase novelty/impact score for first-of-its-kind integrations)
Favors empirical improvements and robust cross-model gains even if primarily feature-based engineering (will score experiments highly if multiple classifiers and held-out tests show consistent gains)
Values demonstrations that difficult linguistic phenomena (irony, sarcasm, thwarting) are better handled—adds credit if targeted evaluations show improvement
Prefers papers that bridge cognitive science and NLP and is lenient about lack of new theory provided the empirical benefit is clear and reproducible
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Prioritizes practical impact from novel modality integration and consistent empirical gains on hard linguistic phenomena; will be generous on novelty and significance when behavioral signals demonstrably improve performance.
